name: NORAD Stand Down
description: The 'NORAD Stand Down' theory alleges that the North American Aerospace Defense Command (NORAD) intentionally failed to intercept or respond adequately to the terrorist attacks on September 11, 2001. Proponents claim this inaction was either due to incompetence, negligence, or, more drastically, a deliberate act to allow the attacks to occur. The theory suggests that NORAD was either complicit in the attacks or deliberately hampered in its response. Evidence cited often includes discrepancies in official timelines, unusual military exercises occurring on the same day, and alleged failures in communication and coordination between different NORAD facilities and air defense assets. Mainstream explanations attribute the delayed response to the unprecedented nature of the attacks, the lack of prior experience dealing with hijacked planes used as weapons, communication breakdowns resulting from the chaos of the situation, and the limitations of NORAD's air defense capabilities at the time, which were primarily geared toward external threats, not internal hijackings. Critiques also point out that NORAD was designed to defend against incoming missiles and bombers, not civilian aircraft, and that the hijackers deliberately timed their actions to exploit vulnerabilities in the existing system. Furthermore, critics highlight the logistical challenges of scrambling fighter jets and intercepting rapidly moving targets within the densely populated northeastern United States.
subtitle: Alleged failure to intercept 9/11 hijackers

claims.claim: NORAD intentionally allowed the 9/11 attacks to occur.
claims.evidence: Delayed response times, conflicting reports on fighter jet deployment, unusual military exercises.
claims.criticism: Lack of concrete evidence, misinterpretations of official reports, normal delays given the unprecedented situation.

claims.claim: Military exercises on 9/11 were a deliberate distraction to hinder NORAD's response.
claims.evidence: Existence of military exercises like 'Vigilant Warrior' and 'Northern Vigilance' that day.
claims.criticism: These exercises are routine, coincidental, and not designed to prevent responses to real-world threats. Many were modified or halted after the attacks.

claims.claim: Communication breakdowns within NORAD were orchestrated to delay the response.
claims.evidence: Reports of communication difficulties and delays in transferring information.
claims.criticism: Communication breakdowns are common in emergency situations and were likely exacerbated by the chaos and stress of the attacks.

keyFigures.name: David Ray Griffin
keyFigures.role: Prominent 9/11 conspiracy theorist who promoted the 'Stand Down' idea.

keyFigures.name: Thomas Kean
keyFigures.role: Chairman of the 9/11 Commission, which investigated the attacks and refuted claims of a deliberate stand down.

timeline.event: Terrorist attacks on the World Trade Center and the Pentagon.
Publication of the 9/11 Commission Report, which addressed and refuted the 'Stand Down' claims.
Continued debate and proliferation of the 'NORAD Stand Down' theory in conspiracy circles.

sources.author: David Ray Griffin
sources.work: The New Pearl Harbor: Disturbing Questions About the Bush Administration and 9/11
sources.title: David Ray Griffin

sources.author: Popular Mechanics
sources.work: Debunking 9/11 Myths: Why Conspiracy Theories Can't Stand Up to the Facts
sources.title: Popular Mechanics
